% =============================================================================
% TITLE
% =============================================================================
\title{%
  \LARGE\textbf{DIFUSCO: Graph-based Diffusion Solvers}\\[0.4em]
  \large for Combinatorial Optimisation\\[0.6em]
  \normalsize Applied to TSP and TSPTW-D
}
\date{\today}

% =============================================================================
\begin{document}
% =============================================================================

\maketitle
\thispagestyle{empty}

\begin{abstract}
This document presents DIFUSCO (Sun \& Yang, NeurIPS 2023), a probabilistic
deep learning framework that formulates combinatorial optimisation as a
\emph{denoising diffusion} problem on graphs.
A Hamiltonian tour is encoded as a binary adjacency matrix
$\mathbf{y}_0 \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$; a learnable graph neural network
(GNN) is trained to reverse a Gaussian diffusion process that progressively
corrupts $\mathbf{y}_0$ into pure noise.
At inference time, the model iteratively denoises a random matrix into a
near-optimal tour.
After a complete mathematical derivation of the DDPM/DDIM forward and reverse
processes, training objective, and architecture, this document reports benchmark
results on standard TSP instances ($n \in \{10, 50, 100\}$) and describes the
extension to TSPTW-D (Travelling Salesman Problem with Time Windows and
Disruptions).
\end{abstract}

\tableofcontents
\newpage

% =============================================================================
\section{Definition}
% =============================================================================

\textbf{DIFUSCO} (\underline{Di}f\underline{fu}sion \underline{S}olvers for
\underline{Co}mbinatorial optimisation) is a deep learning framework that
reframes graph combinatorial problems as \emph{conditional denoising diffusion}
on discrete structures.

\subsection*{Origin and Context}

The method was introduced by Sun \& Yang (2023) and draws on two concurrent
lines of work:

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{cl}
\toprule
\textbf{Year} & \textbf{Contribution} \\
\midrule
2020 & Ho \textit{et al.} --- Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM),
       applied to images \\
2021 & Song \textit{et al.} --- Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM),
       deterministic fast sampling \\
2019 & Joshi \textit{et al.} --- GCN architecture for TSP edge prediction \\
2023 & \textbf{Sun \& Yang} --- DIFUSCO: diffusion process conditioned on graph
       features for CO \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection*{Category}

DIFUSCO belongs to the family of \textbf{probabilistic deep learning solvers}
for combinatorial optimisation.
Unlike deterministic feed-forward models (GNN, Transformer AM), it defines a
\emph{stochastic generative process} over the solution space, which provides
two natural advantages:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item \textbf{Diversity}: different noise samples $\mathbf{y}_T \sim
    \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ produce different candidate tours.
  \item \textbf{Best-of-$N$ sampling}: running $N$ independent reverse chains
    and keeping the shortest tour is trivially parallelisable and improves
    solution quality without retraining.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Key Concepts}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item \textbf{Binary adjacency matrix} $\mathbf{y}_0 \in \{0,1\}^{n\times n}$:
    the tour is represented as $y_{0,ij}=1$ iff edge $(i\to j)$ is in the tour.
  \item \textbf{Continuous relaxation}: the diffusion process treats
    $\mathbf{y}_0$ as a real-valued signal, adding Gaussian noise over $T$ steps.
  \item \textbf{GNN score network} $f_\theta$: a GNN conditioned on graph
    features \emph{and} the current noise level $t$, predicting the clean
    $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$ from the noisy $\mathbf{y}_t$.
  \item \textbf{Sinusoidal time embedding}: a deterministic function of $t$
    gives the network a unique ``fingerprint'' per noise level, analogous to
    Transformer positional encodings.
\end{itemize}

% =============================================================================
\section{Formal Description}
% =============================================================================

\subsection{Problem Formulation}

Let $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ be the complete directed graph
on $n$ nodes with coordinates $\mathbf{x} = \{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^n$,
$\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
Edge distances are $d_{ij} = \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|_2$.

A \textbf{Hamiltonian tour} is encoded as a binary adjacency matrix
$\mathbf{y}_0 \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$ subject to the permutation constraints:
\begin{equation}
  \sum_{j=1}^n y_{0,ij} = 1 \;\forall i,
  \qquad
  \sum_{i=1}^n y_{0,ij} = 1 \;\forall j,
  \qquad
  y_{0,ii} = 0 \;\forall i.
  \label{eq:perm_constraints}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\sum_j y_{0,ij}=1$ & each node has exactly one successor (row sums to 1) \\
  $\sum_i y_{0,ij}=1$ & each node has exactly one predecessor (column sums to 1) \\
  $y_{0,ii}=0$        & no self-loops (diagonal is zero) \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

The TSP objective is:
\begin{equation}
  \min_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{H}_n}
  \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n d_{ij}\, y_{ij}
  \label{eq:tsp_obj}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathcal{H}_n$ & set of all valid Hamiltonian adjacency matrices \\
  $d_{ij}$        & Euclidean distance between nodes $i$ and $j$ \\
  $y_{ij}\in\{0,1\}$ & 1 if edge $(i\!\to\!j)$ is used in the tour, 0 otherwise \\
  $\sum_{ij} d_{ij}y_{ij}$ & total tour length (objective to minimise) \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsection{Forward Diffusion Process}

\subsubsection*{Noise Schedule}

A linear variance schedule $\{\beta_t\}_{t=1}^T$ is defined as:
\begin{equation}
  \beta_t = \beta_{\min} + \frac{t-1}{T-1}(\beta_{\max} - \beta_{\min}),
  \qquad
  \beta_{\min} = 10^{-4},\; \beta_{\max} = 0.02,\; T = 1000.
\end{equation}

where $\beta_t \in (0,1)$ is the \textbf{noise variance added at step $t$},
growing linearly from nearly zero to $0.02$ as $t$ increases from $1$ to $T$.

The derived cumulative quantities are:
\begin{equation}
  \alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t,
  \qquad
  \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t \alpha_s.
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\alpha_t$        & signal retention at step $t$; fraction of previous signal kept ($\alpha_t < 1$) \\
  $\bar{\alpha}_t$  & cumulative retention up to step $t$; fraction of $\mathbf{y}_0$ surviving after $t$ steps \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
$\bar{\alpha}_t$ decreases monotonically from $\bar{\alpha}_1 \approx 1$
(almost no corruption) to $\bar{\alpha}_T \approx 0$ (pure noise).

\subsubsection*{One-step Markov Kernel}

The forward process corrupts the signal one step at a time via a Gaussian kernel:
\begin{equation}
  q(\mathbf{y}_t \mid \mathbf{y}_{t-1})
  = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_t;\;
    \sqrt{\alpha_t}\,\mathbf{y}_{t-1},\;
    (1-\alpha_t)\,\mathbf{I}\right).
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y};\boldsymbol{\mu},\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ & Gaussian distribution with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ \\
  $\sqrt{\alpha_t}$  & attenuation factor; scales the signal down by $<1$ each step \\
  $(1-\alpha_t)$     & variance of the fresh Gaussian noise added at this step \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{Reparameterisation Trick (direct sampling)}

By composing $t$ Gaussian kernels, one can sample $\mathbf{y}_t$
\emph{directly from $\mathbf{y}_0$} without iterating all intermediate steps:
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{
    q(\mathbf{y}_t \mid \mathbf{y}_0)
    = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_t;\;
      \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_0,\;
      (1-\bar{\alpha}_t)\,\mathbf{I}\right)
  }
  \label{eq:forward_closed}
\end{equation}

equivalently written as a \textbf{sampling formula}:
\begin{equation}
  \mathbf{y}_t
  = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_0
  + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon},
  \qquad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I}).
  \label{eq:reparam}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathbf{y}_t$            & noisy tour matrix at diffusion step $t$ \\
  $\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}$   & surviving signal weight (decreases as $t$ increases toward 0) \\
  $\mathbf{y}_0$            & clean binary tour adjacency matrix \\
  $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$ & noise weight (increases as $t$ increases toward 1) \\
  $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$& standard Gaussian noise sample \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
This is the \textbf{reparameterisation trick}: $\mathbf{y}_t$ is a linear
interpolation between the clean tour $\mathbf{y}_0$ and pure noise
$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, with mixing weights controlled by $\bar{\alpha}_t$.

\paragraph{Proof by induction.}
Composing $q(\mathbf{y}_t\mid\mathbf{y}_{t-1})$ and
$q(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}
\mathbf{y}_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})\mathbf{I})$ yields a Gaussian with
mean $\sqrt{\alpha_t\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\,\mathbf{y}_0 = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_0$
and variance
$\alpha_t(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})+(1-\alpha_t) = 1-\bar{\alpha}_t$.

\subsection{Reverse Denoising Process}

\subsubsection*{Reverse Markov Chain}

The reverse process reconstructs $\mathbf{y}_0$ from
$\mathbf{y}_T \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ by applying a sequence
of learned denoising steps:
\begin{equation}
  p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_{0:T})
  = p(\mathbf{y}_T)
    \prod_{t=1}^T
    p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{y}_t, \mathbf{x}).
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $p(\mathbf{y}_T)$ & prior: standard Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ \\
  $p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t,\mathbf{x})$ & one learned denoising step, conditioned on graph features $\mathbf{x}$ \\
  $\theta$          & learnable parameters of the GNN score network \\
  $\mathbf{x}$      & graph-level features (node coordinates, time windows, etc.) \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{Analytic Forward Posterior}

The key identity in DDPM is that the \emph{forward posterior}
$q(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t, \mathbf{y}_0)$ ---
``given that we know both the noisy $\mathbf{y}_t$ and the clean $\mathbf{y}_0$,
what does the intermediate $\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$ look like?'' ---
is analytically tractable (Gaussian):
\begin{equation}
  q(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t, \mathbf{y}_0)
  = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_{t-1};\;
    \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t,\;
    \tilde{\beta}_t\,\mathbf{I}\right)
  \label{eq:forward_posterior}
\end{equation}

with posterior mean and variance derived via Bayes' rule:
\begin{align}
  \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t
    &= \frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\,\beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_0
     + \frac{\sqrt{\alpha_t}(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_t,
  \label{eq:posterior_mean}\\[6pt]
  \tilde{\beta}_t
    &= \frac{(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})\,\beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}.
  \label{eq:posterior_var}
\end{align}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t$ & posterior mean: weighted blend of $\mathbf{y}_0$ and $\mathbf{y}_t$ \\
  $\tilde{\beta}_t$            & posterior variance; $\to 0$ as $t\to 1$ (increasing certainty) \\
  $c_1 = \frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$ & weight on the clean tour $\mathbf{y}_0$; grows as $t$ decreases \\
  $c_2 = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha_t}(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$ & weight on the noisy matrix $\mathbf{y}_t$; dominates at large $t$ \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{DDPM Reverse Step}

Since $\mathbf{y}_0$ is unknown at inference, the GNN predicts it:
$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 = f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t, t, \mathbf{x})$.
Substituting the prediction into the analytic posterior gives the
\textbf{parameterised reverse distribution}:
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{
    p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t, \mathbf{x})
    = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_{t-1};\;
      c_1\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 + c_2\,\mathbf{y}_t,\;
      \tilde{\beta}_t\,\mathbf{I}\right)
  }
  \label{eq:ddpm_reverse}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $c_1\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$ & predicted clean tour contribution (uses $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 = f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t,t,\mathbf{x})$) \\
  $c_2\,\mathbf{y}_t$       & current noisy state contribution \\
  $\tilde{\beta}_t$         & residual uncertainty (posterior variance from Eq.~\ref{eq:posterior_var}) \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
Sampling: $\mathbf{y}_{t-1} = c_1\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 + c_2\mathbf{y}_t + \sqrt{\tilde{\beta}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$,\;
$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ freshly drawn to avoid deterministic collapse.

\subsubsection*{DDIM Deterministic Accelerated Sampling}

Song \textit{et al.} (2021) reformulate the reverse as a \textbf{deterministic
non-Markovian process} that can skip timesteps:
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{
    \mathbf{y}_{t-1}
    = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0
    + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\,
      \frac{\mathbf{y}_t - \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}}
  }
  \label{eq:ddim}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$          & updated (less noisy) matrix \\
  $\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$ & target signal component \\
  $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$        & predicted clean tour from $f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t,t,\mathbf{x})$ \\
  $\dfrac{\mathbf{y}_t-\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}}$ & noise direction: unit vector from predicted clean tour toward $\mathbf{y}_t$ \\
  $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}$ & target noise weight at the previous timestep \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
This unit noise direction is projected onto the target noise level of the
previous timestep, producing a consistent $\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$ without any
stochastic injection.
This enables \textbf{large step-size skipping}: instead of iterating all $T=1000$
steps, only $S=50$ evenly-spaced steps are needed (\textbf{20$\times$ faster}).

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
& \textbf{DDPM} & \textbf{DDIM} \\
\midrule
Required steps & $T = 1000$ & $S = 50$ \\
Stochastic & Yes ($+\sqrt{\tilde{\beta}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$) & No ($\eta = 0$) \\
Step-size flexibility & No & Yes (arbitrary sub-sequence) \\
Quality at $S=50$ & degraded & good \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Training Objective}

\subsubsection*{Variational Lower Bound (ELBO)}

Maximising $\log p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$ is intractable;
instead one maximises the Evidence Lower BOund (ELBO):
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}
  = \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[-\log p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_0\mid\mathbf{y}_1)\right]
  + \sum_{t=2}^T \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[
      D_\mathrm{KL}\!\left(
        q(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t,\mathbf{y}_0)
        \;\|\;
        p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}\mid\mathbf{y}_t,\mathbf{x})
      \right)
    \right]
  + D_\mathrm{KL}\!\left(q(\mathbf{y}_T\mid\mathbf{y}_0)\;\|\;p(\mathbf{y}_T)\right).
  \label{eq:elbo}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathbb{E}_q[-\log p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_0\mid\mathbf{y}_1)]$ & reconstruction term: quality of the final denoising step \\
  $\sum_t D_\mathrm{KL}(q\|p_\theta)$ & denoising terms: learned reverse must match the analytic forward posterior at each step \\
  $D_\mathrm{KL}(q(\mathbf{y}_T\mid\mathbf{y}_0)\|p(\mathbf{y}_T))$ & prior matching term; constant w.r.t.\ $\theta$, ignored during training \\
  $D_\mathrm{KL}(q\|p)$ & KL divergence measuring how far $p_\theta$ deviates from $q$; both are Gaussian here, so each KL reduces to $\|\mathbf{y}_0-\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0\|^2$ up to a scalar \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{Simplified Loss (Ho \textit{et al.}, 2020)}

Dropping the per-timestep weighting and sampling $t$ uniformly gives the
simplified objective:
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{simple}
  = \mathbb{E}_{t,\,\mathbf{y}_0,\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}
    \left[\left\|\mathbf{y}_0 - f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t, t, \mathbf{x})\right\|^2\right].
  \label{eq:l2_loss}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $t$                     & timestep sampled uniformly from $\{1,\ldots,T\}$ \\
  $\mathbf{y}_0$          & clean tour label from the training set \\
  $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ & noise used to compute $\mathbf{y}_t$ via Eq.~\ref{eq:reparam} \\
  $f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t,t,\mathbf{x})$ & model prediction of the clean tour from the noisy state \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{Binary Cross-Entropy (DIFUSCO choice)}

Since $y_{0,ij} \in \{0,1\}$, DIFUSCO treats the prediction as a binary
classification problem and uses Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE):
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{
    \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{BCE}
    = -\,\mathbb{E}_{t,(i,j)}\!
      \left[
        y_{0,ij}\,\log\hat{y}_{0,ij}
        + (1-y_{0,ij})\,\log(1-\hat{y}_{0,ij})
      \right]
  }
  \label{eq:bce}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $y_{0,ij}\in\{0,1\}$  & true label for edge $(i,j)$: 1 if in the tour, 0 otherwise \\
  $\hat{y}_{0,ij}\in(0,1)$ & predicted probability: $\sigma(f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t,t,\mathbf{x})_{ij})$ where $\sigma(z)=1/(1+e^{-z})$ \\
  $y_{0,ij}\log\hat{y}_{0,ij}$ & positive-edge term; large penalty when model predicts near 0 for a true edge \\
  $(1-y_{0,ij})\log(1-\hat{y}_{0,ij})$ & negative-edge term; large penalty when model predicts near 1 for a false edge \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsection{Architecture: DifuscoModel}

\subsubsection*{Sinusoidal Time Embedding}

The discrete timestep $t \in \{1,\ldots,T\}$ is mapped to a $d$-dimensional
vector using the Transformer positional encoding formula:
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{TimeEmb}(t)_{2k}   = \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2k/d}}\right),
  \quad
  \mathrm{TimeEmb}(t)_{2k+1} = \cos\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2k/d}}\right),
  \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, \tfrac{d}{2}-1.
  \label{eq:time_emb}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $t$              & current diffusion timestep $\in\{1,\ldots,T\}$ \\
  $d$              & embedding dimension (matches GNN hidden size) \\
  $k$              & frequency band index ($0$ to $d/2-1$) \\
  $10000^{2k/d}$   & frequency period, growing exponentially with $k$: low $k$ = slow/coarse signal, high $k$ = fast/fine signal \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
The resulting vector is unique for every $t\in[1,T]$, giving the network a
smooth, injective fingerprint of the current noise level.

\subsubsection*{DifuscoGNNLayer}

Each of the $L$ GNN layers follows the Joshi \textit{et al.} (2019) architecture
augmented with a timestep conditioning branch.
Given node embeddings $\mathbf{h}_i^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and edge embeddings
$\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ at layer $l$:

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{1. Time conditioning.}
The time embedding is projected to edge dimension:
\begin{equation}
  \mathbf{t}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}_t^{(l)}\,\mathrm{TimeEmb}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^d
\end{equation}

where $\mathbf{W}_t^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ is a learnable weight
matrix that adapts the timestep signal to the scale of the edge embeddings.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{2. Edge update.}
\begin{equation}
  \mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l+1)} = \mathrm{BN}\!\left(\sigma\!\left(
    \mathbf{W}_e^{(l)}\!\left[
      \mathbf{h}_i^{(l)}\;\|\;
      \mathbf{h}_j^{(l)}\;\|\;
      \mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)}\;\|\;
      \mathbf{t}^{(l)}
    \right]\right)\right)
  \label{eq:difusco_edge}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathbf{h}_i^{(l)}$   & source node embedding at layer $l$ \\
  $\mathbf{h}_j^{(l)}$   & target node embedding at layer $l$ \\
  $\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)}$& previous edge embedding (from layer $l$) \\
  $\mathbf{t}^{(l)}$     & projected timestep signal at layer $l$ \\
  $[\cdot\|\cdot]$       & vector concatenation; the 4 vectors together give the network full context about edge $(i\!\to\!j)$ at noise level $t$ \\
  $\mathbf{W}_e^{(l)}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times 4d}$ & learnable projection; $\sigma$ = ReLU; $\mathrm{BN}$ = Batch Normalisation \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{3. Attention-weighted node update.}
\begin{equation}
  a_{ij}^{(l)} = \mathrm{softmax}_j\!\left(\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l+1)}\right),
  \qquad
  \mathbf{h}_i^{(l+1)} = \mathrm{BN}\!\left(\sigma\!\left(
    \mathbf{W}_n^{(l)} \sum_j a_{ij}^{(l)} \odot \mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l+1)}
  \right)\right)
  \label{eq:difusco_node}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $a_{ij}^{(l)}$ & attention weight on edge $(i\!\to\!j)$, normalised over all outgoing neighbours $j$ \\
  $\mathrm{softmax}_j$ & normalises attention weights over all $j$ neighbours of node $i$ \\
  $a_{ij}^{(l)}\odot\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l+1)}$ & edge embedding weighted by attention ($\odot$ = element-wise multiply) \\
  $\mathbf{W}_n^{(l)}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ & learnable node projection applied to the aggregated neighbourhood \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{4. Output head.}
After $L$ layers, a single linear layer and sigmoid produce the predicted
probability that each edge belongs to the clean tour:
\begin{equation}
  \hat{y}_{0,ij}
  = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o\,\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(L)}\right) \in (0,1).
  \label{eq:difusco_out}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(L)}$ & final edge embedding after $L$ GNN layers \\
  $\mathbf{W}_o$          & learnable linear projection (output head) \\
  $\sigma$                & sigmoid $\sigma(z)=1/(1+e^{-z})$, mapping to $(0,1)$ \\
  $\hat{y}_{0,ij}$        & predicted probability that edge $(i,j)$ belongs to the clean tour \\
\end{tabular}}\smallskip

\subsubsection*{Input Features}

The edge embedding input to the first layer concatenates two components:
\begin{equation}
  \mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(0),\mathrm{in}}
  = \left[\;\phi_{ij}^{\mathrm{edge}},\; y_{t,ij}\;\right] \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e+1}
  \label{eq:edge_input}
\end{equation}
{\small\begin{tabular}{@{\hspace{1.5em}}l@{\;---\;}p{9.5cm}}
  $\phi_{ij}^{\mathrm{edge}}$ & static graph features (e.g.\ Euclidean distance; worst-case travel time for TSPTW-D) \\
  $y_{t,ij}$     & current value of the noisy adjacency matrix at step $t$ (the diffusion state) \\
  $d_e$          & number of graph-level edge features \\
\end{tabular}}

\smallskip
The key difference from a plain GNN is the $y_{t,ij}$ term: it injects the
diffusion state directly into the edge stream, letting the network see how
corrupted the tour is at each step and adjust its denoising accordingly.

\subsubsection*{Model Presets}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Preset} & $d$ & $L$ & $T$ & \textbf{Parameters} \\
\midrule
\texttt{small}  & 64  & 4 & 500  & $\sim 119{,}000$ \\
\texttt{medium} & 128 & 6 & 1000 & $\sim 669{,}000$ \\
\texttt{large}  & 256 & 8 & 1000 & $\sim 2{,}900{,}000$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Complexity}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Component} & \textbf{Time} & \textbf{Space} & \textbf{Dominant term} \\
\midrule
Input encoding          & $O(n^2 d)$     & $O(n^2 d)$    & edges \\
GNN layer ($\times L$)  & $O(n^2 d^2)$   & $O(n^2 d)$    & edges \\
Output head             & $O(n^2 d)$     & $O(n^2)$      & --- \\
Reverse chain ($S$ steps) & $O(S n^2 d^2)$ & $O(n^2 d)$  & diffusion steps \\
Greedy decoder          & $O(n^2)$       & $O(n^2)$      & --- \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total}          & $\boldsymbol{O(S\,L\,n^2 d^2)}$
                        & $\boldsymbol{O(n^2 d)}$
                        & \textbf{edges $\times$ steps} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The bottleneck is the \textbf{same $O(n^2 d)$ edge embedding tensor} as the GNN
(both share the Joshi architecture), with the additional multiplicative factor $S$
from the diffusion chain.
For the default $S=50$ (DDIM), this is a $50\times$ overhead versus a single GNN
forward pass, partially offset by the best-of-$N$ quality gains.

% =============================================================================
\section{Architecture / Algorithm Steps}
% =============================================================================

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{l}
\textbf{Training (one step)} \\
$\quad$ Sample instance $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}_0)$ from dataset \\
$\quad$ Sample $t \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(\{1,\ldots,T\})$ and
        $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ \\
$\quad\downarrow$ \\
$\quad$ Compute $\mathbf{y}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{y}_0
        + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$
        \quad\textit{(reparameterisation, Eq.~\ref{eq:reparam})} \\
$\quad\downarrow$ \\
$\quad$ Predict $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 = \sigma(f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t, t, \mathbf{x}))$
        \quad\textit{(forward pass through $L$ DifuscoGNNLayers)} \\
$\quad\downarrow$ \\
$\quad$ Compute $\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{BCE}(\mathbf{y}_0, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_0)$
        and back-propagate \\[1em]
\textbf{Inference (DDIM, $S$ steps)} \\
$\quad$ Sample $\mathbf{y}_T \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ \\
$\quad$ For $t = T, T{-}\Delta, \ldots, 1$: \\
$\quad\quad$ Predict $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 = \sigma(f_\theta(\mathbf{y}_t, t, \mathbf{x}))$ \\
$\quad\quad$ Compute noise direction $\mathbf{d} =
  (\mathbf{y}_t - \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0)
  / \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$ \\
$\quad\quad$ Update $\mathbf{y}_{t-\Delta} =
  \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-\Delta}}\,\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0
  + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-\Delta}}\,\mathbf{d}$
  \quad\textit{(Eq.~\ref{eq:ddim})} \\
$\quad$ Greedy decode $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0 \to$ tour $\pi$
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Best-of-$N$ sampling.}
Run $N$ independent reverse chains from different
$\mathbf{y}_T^{(k)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$,
decode each to a tour $\pi^{(k)}$, and return
$\arg\min_k \mathrm{length}(\pi^{(k)})$.
This is \textbf{embarrassingly parallel} and reduces the optimality gap
significantly with $N \in \{4, 8\}$ without any retraining.

% =============================================================================
\section{TSPTW-D Extension}
% =============================================================================

\subsection{Problem Definition}

The \textbf{Travelling Salesman Problem with Time Windows and Disruptions
(TSPTW-D)} extends the TSP with:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item \textbf{Time window} $[a_i, b_i]$ per client $i$: service must begin
    within this interval.
  \item \textbf{Service duration} $s_i$: time spent at client $i$.
  \item \textbf{Disruptions} $\{\delta_{ij}\}$: stochastic travel-time
    perturbations on edge $(i,j)$.
\end{itemize}

The \textbf{extended objective} penalises time-window violations:
\begin{equation}
  \min_{\pi \in \mathcal{H}_n}\left[
    \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} d_{\pi(k),\pi(k+1)}
    + \lambda\sum_{i=1}^n \max\!\left(0,\; T_i^{\mathrm{arr}} - b_i\right)
  \right]
  \label{eq:tsptwd_obj}
\end{equation}
where $T_i^{\mathrm{arr}}$ is the arrival time at client $i$ along tour $\pi$.

\subsection{Feature Encoding}

Each node $i$ is encoded with 5 normalised features ($T_{\max} = 200$ min):
\begin{equation}
  \phi_i^{\mathrm{node}}
  = \left(x_i,\; y_i,\; \frac{a_i}{T_{\max}},\; \frac{b_i}{T_{\max}},\;
    \frac{s_i}{T_{\max}}\right) \in [0,1]^5.
  \label{eq:node_feats}
\end{equation}

Each edge $(i,j)$ is encoded with the worst-case travel time under known
perturbations:
\begin{equation}
  \phi_{ij}^{\mathrm{edge}}
  = d_{ij} + \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{P}_{ij}} \delta \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}.
  \label{eq:edge_feats}
\end{equation}
This alerts the model to potentially dangerous arcs before the tour is
constructed.

% =============================================================================
\section{Strengths and Limitations}
% =============================================================================

\subsection*{Strengths}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{9.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Property} & \textbf{Detail} \\
\midrule
Probabilistic & Defines a distribution over tours; diversity via different
  $\mathbf{y}_T$ seeds \\
Best-of-$N$ & Trivially run $N$ chains in parallel; quality improves
  monotonically with $N$ \\
Graph-conditioned & GNN explicitly models edge structure; same inductive bias
  as TSP-GNN \\
Time-aware & Sinusoidal embedding gives the network different denoising
  behaviour at each noise level \\
Flexible inference & DDIM allows trading quality for speed by choosing $S$ \\
TSPTW-D ready & Feature encoding directly accommodates time windows,
  service times, and disruptions \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection*{Limitations}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{9.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Limitation} & \textbf{Impact} \\
\midrule
Quadratic memory & Same $O(n^2 d)$ edge bottleneck as GNN; infeasible for
  $n \gtrsim 500$ \\
Slow inference vs.\ GNN & $S = 50$ reverse steps vs.\ 1 forward pass;
  ${\sim}50\times$ slower per sample \\
No hard constraint enforcement & Greedy decode may produce tours that violate
  TW constraints; post-processing needed \\
Requires tour labels & Supervised training needs heuristic labels
  (NN or 2-opt); RL variant is possible but harder \\
Continuous relaxation & Binary $\{0,1\}$ targets in a continuous diffusion
  process: the model must learn to produce near-binary $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

% =============================================================================
\section{Use Case Explanation}
% =============================================================================

\subsection*{When to Use DIFUSCO}

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
  \item \textbf{Multiple high-quality solutions are needed.}
    Best-of-$N$ sampling at inference time produces an ensemble of tours
    without retraining.

  \item \textbf{Problem instances follow a repeating distribution.}
    As with the GNN, training cost is amortised over many inferences.

  \item \textbf{Stochastic constraints require robustness.}
    The probabilistic reverse process can implicitly hedge against perturbations
    when trained on disrupted instances.

  \item $\boldsymbol{n \leq 300}$ \textbf{(practical limit without sparse graphs).}
    Beyond this, the $O(n^2)$ edge tensor dominates.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{DIFUSCO vs.\ GNN vs.\ Classical Solvers}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lcccl}
\toprule
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Gap ($n=100$)} & \textbf{Speed} &
  \textbf{Diversity} & \textbf{Memory} \\
\midrule
Nearest-Neighbour        & ${\sim}20\,\%$ & very fast & no  & $O(n^2)$ \\
GNN (Joshi 2019)         & $1$--$3\,\%$   & fast       & no  & $O(Ln^2d)$ \\
\textbf{DIFUSCO $\times$1} & $1$--$3\,\%$ & slow ($50\times$ GNN) & yes & $O(n^2d)$ \\
\textbf{DIFUSCO $\times$8} & $<1\,\%$     & $8\times$ slower & yes & $O(n^2d)$ \\
LKH-3                    & $<0.1\,\%$     & seconds    & yes & $O(n^2)$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

% =============================================================================
\section{Code Implementation}
% =============================================================================

The full model is defined in \texttt{model.py}; training and sampling in
\texttt{train.py}; data loading in \texttt{data.py}.

\begin{lstlisting}[style=pythonstyle,
  caption={Load model, run DDIM inference, decode tour
           (\texttt{model.py} + \texttt{train.py})}]
import torch
from model import DifuscoModel, MODEL_SIZES
from train import NoiseSchedule, sample, sample_best_of
from data  import load_dataset, greedy_decode

DEVICE = 'mps'   # or 'cuda' / 'cpu'
SIZE   = 'medium'
d, L, T = MODEL_SIZES[SIZE]

# --- Load trained model ---------------------------------------------------
model    = DifuscoModel(d=d, L=L, node_dim=5, edge_dim=1)
schedule = NoiseSchedule(T=T)
model.load_state_dict(
    torch.load(f'model/difusco_{SIZE}.pt', map_location='cpu')
)
model.eval()

# --- Load a TSPTW-D instance ----------------------------------------------
ds = load_dataset(n=20)      # dict: coords, node_feats, edge_feats, ...

# --- Single DDIM sample ---------------------------------------------------
p_hat = sample(model, ds['node_feats'], schedule,
               edge_extra=ds['edge_feats'],
               n_steps=50, ddim=True, device=DEVICE)

tour = greedy_decode(p_hat.cpu())   # list of node indices

# --- Best of 8 samples (better quality) -----------------------------------
tour_best, length_best, _ = sample_best_of(
    model, ds['node_feats'], schedule,
    edge_extra=ds['edge_feats'],
    n_samples=8, n_steps=50, device=DEVICE
)
print(f'Best tour length: {length_best:.4f}')
\end{lstlisting}

\begin{lstlisting}[style=bashstyle,
  caption={Training pipeline (run from \texttt{DL\_MODEL/difusco/})}]
# Full curriculum (small -> large instances, 3 stages)
bash train.sh small

# With 2-opt labels (slower but better label quality)
bash train.sh medium --two_opt

# Train TSP-only model (for documentation benchmark)
python train.py --mode tsp --size medium --n 50 --epochs 3000
\end{lstlisting}

The training script applies a \textbf{curriculum} across four stages:

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{clccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Stage} & \textbf{Instance sizes} & \textbf{Label} &
  \textbf{Steps} & \textbf{LR} \\
\midrule
1 & $n=10$, pre-generated dataset & 2-opt & 3\,000 & $10^{-3}$ \\
2 & $n\in\{20,50,100\}$, random & NN or 2-opt & 3\,000 & $5\!\times\!10^{-4}$ \\
3 & $n\in\{200,300\}$, random & NN & 1\,000 & $10^{-4}$ \\
4 & $n\in\{500,1000\}$, random & NN & 300 & $5\!\times\!10^{-5}$ \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\small Stage 4 requires significant VRAM ($O(n^2)$
  edge tensor + diffusion).}\\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

% =============================================================================
\section{TSP Benchmark}
% =============================================================================

The benchmark uses the \textbf{medium} preset ($d=128$, $L=6$, $T=1000$,
${\sim}669\mathrm{k}$ parameters) trained with the Stage~1--2 curriculum
and evaluated with DDIM ($S=50$) on randomly generated instances.
The \textbf{optimal} reference for $n=10$ is the brute-force solution
($9! = 362{,}880$ permutations); for $n \geq 50$, the nearest-neighbour
heuristic serves as reference.

\subsection*{Metrics}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{10cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{Definition} \\
\midrule
Avg.\ tour length & Mean total Euclidean length over $N_{\mathrm{trials}}$ random instances \\
Gap (\%) & $(L_{\mathrm{method}} / L_{\mathrm{ref}} - 1) \times 100\,\%$ \\
Inference time & Mean wall-clock time per instance (model forward pass
  + greedy decode) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\cmidrule(lr){4-5}
& & & CPU & MPS/GPU \\
\midrule
Optimal          & --- & 0.0\,\%   & (BF) & --- \\

    BCE training loss curve for the medium TSP model.
    The smoothed moving average (navy) reveals convergence behaviour;

  }

    brute-force optimal (green).

  }

% =============================================================================
\section{Scalability Analysis}
\label{sec:scalability}
% =============================================================================

DIFUSCO inherits the same $O(n^2 d)$ memory bottleneck as the GNN,
with an additional multiplicative factor $S$ from the diffusion chain.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{ccccl}
\toprule
$n$ & Edge memory ($d=128$, float32) & GNN time & DIFUSCO time ($S=50$)
  & Feasible? \\
\midrule
50    & ${\sim}1.3$\,MB & $<1$\,ms  & ${\sim}50$\,ms  & Yes \\
100   & ${\sim}5$\,MB   & $<5$\,ms  & ${\sim}250$\,ms & Yes \\
200   & ${\sim}20$\,MB  & $<20$\,ms & ${\sim}1$\,s    & Yes \\
300   & ${\sim}46$\,MB  & $<50$\,ms & ${\sim}2.5$\,s  & Marginal \\
500   & ${\sim}128$\,MB & $<150$\,ms & ${\sim}7.5$\,s & Borderline \\
1\,000 & ${\sim}512$\,MB & $<600$\,ms & ${\sim}30$\,s & GPU only \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Projected scalability for the medium preset ($d=128$, float32,
  $S=50$ DDIM steps).
  DIFUSCO time $\approx S \times$ GNN time.
  Memory is independent of $S$ (the $n\times n$ tensor is reused in-place).}
\label{tab:scalability}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Conclusion.}
The dense DIFUSCO is practical for $n \leq 300$ on a modern GPU.
For larger instances, the same sparse $k$-NN approximation used for GNNs
reduces memory to $O(knd)$ --- linear in $n$ --- at the cost of potentially
missing non-adjacent optimal edges.

% =============================================================================
\section{Experimental Analysis}
% =============================================================================

\paragraph{BCE loss plateau.}
At initialisation, the model outputs near-$0.5$ predictions, yielding
$\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{BCE} \approx \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
Convergence is visible as the loss drops below $0.3$--$0.4$ and the predicted
$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_0$ becomes sparse (near-binary).

\paragraph{Denoising progression.}
During the reverse chain, early steps ($t$ large) produce blurred,
nearly-uniform matrices; middle steps sharpen the tour skeleton;
final steps refine individual edge decisions.
This can be visualised from the \texttt{benchmark\_tsptwd.ipynb} Section~5
denoising heatmaps.

\paragraph{Best-of-$N$ quality gain.}
Empirically, the gap to optimal decreases roughly as $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ with the
number of independent samples, consistent with order-statistics theory for
i.i.d.\ approximately-Gaussian tour lengths.
$N=8$ provides most of the gain; beyond $N=16$ returns diminish.

\paragraph{DDIM step sensitivity.}
Quality degrades gracefully from $S=50$ to $S=20$ (roughly $+0.5\,\%$ gap)
but collapses at $S \leq 5$.
$S=50$ is the recommended default.

\paragraph{Comparison with GNN.}
DIFUSCO$\times$1 achieves similar quality to the GNN at $50\times$ the
inference cost.
The advantage emerges with DIFUSCO$\times$8: the best-of-8 tour is typically
$1$--$2\,\%$ shorter than the GNN at the cost of $400\times$ the computation,
which is acceptable in batch-processing scenarios.
